text: Using a preferred stride length (or up to 3% shorter than preferred) is most economical for running economy
specific_value: up to 3% shorter than preferred stride length

text: Lower vertical oscillation during running is associated with better running economy
Greater leg stiffness is associated with better running economy
Less leg extension at toe-off and larger stride angles are associated with better running economy
Maintaining arm swing during running improves running economy compared to restricting arm movement
Lower muscular coactivation of thigh antagonist-agonist muscles and lower activation of lower limb muscles during propulsion are associated with better running economy
Being barefoot or wearing lightweight shoes and running on firm, compliant surfaces are associated with better running economy
The propulsion phase of ground contact has the strongest direct links with running economy among all gait phases
